title: Adjacency Matrix
description: A 2D grid of yes/no edges: O(1) edge lookups, but O(V²) space regardless of how many edges actually exist.
objectives: Build an adjacency matrix for a graph
Check and add edges in O(1)
Know when a matrix is the right representation choice

sections.text: Graph Representation previewed the matrix as "a 2D grid — matrix[i][j] tells you whether an edge exists." Let's build one.
For a directed graph (recall Graphs), only matrix[from][to] would be set — not the reverse. matrix[i][j] == 1 means "an edge exists from i to j"; 0 means it doesn't.
Recall Arrays' O(1) indexed access. matrix[i][j] is a direct index into a 2D array — checking whether an edge exists, or adding one, is a single O(1) operation, no matter how large the graph is.

The matrix always allocates vertices × vertices cells, regardless of how many edges actually exist — recall Space Complexity. A graph with 1,000 vertices but only 5 actual edges still pays for a 1,000,000-cell matrix, with the overwhelming majority sitting at 0. This is the direct cost previewed in Graph Representation.

An adjacency matrix shines for dense graphs (many edges relative to vertices) and whenever "does an edge exist between these two specific vertices?" needs to be checked frequently and quickly — the O(1) lookup is genuinely valuable there. For sparse graphs (relatively few edges), the wasted space usually isn't worth it.

sections.title: A round-robin tournament's full results grid
sections.text: A round-robin tournament, where every team plays every other team, naturally fits a grid: row i, column j tells you the result between team i and team j — every cell is meaningful, nothing wasted, because the tournament genuinely is densely connected. That's exactly when a matrix earns its space cost.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to set both matrix[i][j] AND matrix[j][i] for an undirected edge — only setting one direction silently creates a directed edge instead.
Using a matrix for a large, sparse graph — the O(V²) space cost can become enormous compared to the actual number of edges.
Assuming matrix[i][j] == 0 always means 'no edge exists in either direction' — for a directed graph, it only means no edge from i to j specifically.
Forgetting the matrix's diagonal (matrix[i][i]) represents a self-loop, not something to leave undefined by accident.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does an adjacency matrix represent a graph?
questions.answer: As a 2D grid where matrix[i][j] indicates whether an edge exists from vertex i to vertex j.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of checking whether an edge exists in an adjacency matrix?
questions.answer: O(1) — a direct index lookup, regardless of the graph's size.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of an adjacency matrix, and why can this be wasteful?
questions.answer: O(V²), where V is the number of vertices, regardless of how many edges actually exist — for a sparse graph with few edges relative to vertices, most of the matrix is wasted zeros.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An adjacency matrix represents a graph as a 2D grid, where matrix[i][j] indicates whether an edge exists from vertex i to vertex j — for an undirected graph, both matrix[i][j] and matrix[j][i] are set. Checking or adding an edge is O(1), a direct index lookup, but the matrix always costs O(V²) space regardless of how many edges actually exist, making it a poor fit for sparse graphs with relatively few edges. It's the right choice for dense graphs, or whenever frequent, fast edge-existence checks between specific vertex pairs matter most.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Adjacency Matrix
sections.text: Adjacency matrix = a round-robin tournament grid, everyone vs everyone. O(1) edge check, O(V²) space no matter how sparse the real connections are.
